title: Low beams win at night
summary: The right headlight setting keeps the road readable without washing it out.
body: Correct answer: use low beams when you need to cut glare and keep vision intact.
Why: high glare can erase the edge of the road and the shape of other vehicles.
Trap: glare trap makes the brightest light feel like the safest one.
Reclaim: lower the beam, look for edges, and keep the scan soft.

title: Wet roads need more space
summary: Water changes braking and steering so following distance matters more.
body: Correct answer: increase distance and brake earlier on wet pavement.
Why: water stretches both reaction and stopping distance.
Trap: wet-stop overconfidence assumes friction stays the same.
Reclaim: leave more room and verify grip before asking for a stop.

title: Hydroplaning is loss of contact
summary: When water gets between tire and road, the car stops reading the surface.
body: Correct answer: hydroplaning is a loss of tire contact on water.
Why: if the tire cannot touch the road, it cannot steer or brake normally.
Trap: the road may look visible while the contact patch disappears.
Reclaim: slow down, reduce inputs, and give the tires time to recover.

title: Pedestrians hide in shadow
summary: Dark edges and bus stops can conceal movement that deserves caution.
body: Correct answer: slow and scan for movement near the curb or bus stop.
Why: shadows hide people better than they hide danger.
Trap: shadow misread assumes an empty dark patch is truly empty.
Reclaim: widen the scan, expect movement, and keep speed low.

title: Bike lanes need night caution
summary: A bike lane at night is still active space that can contain unseen riders.
body: Correct answer: keep scanning the lane edge and ride your lane cleanly.
Why: darkness makes a bike lane easy to misread.
Trap: lane drift at night comes from assuming the edge is empty.
Reclaim: anchor on markers and keep the wheel calm.

title: Fatigue demands a slower scan
summary: Tired eyes and a tired brain need more structure, not more speed.
body: Correct answer: slow down, widen the scan, and stop if needed.
Why: fatigue narrows attention before you notice it has done so.
Trap: fatigue blur tells you everything is fine while the scan collapses.
Reclaim: reduce load and reset before the misses stack up.

title: Signals must announce early
summary: A signal only works when other road users can react in time.
body: Correct answer: signal before the maneuver, not during it.
Why: a late signal is information after the decision is already made.
Trap: late signaling is the night version of asking for trust too late.
Reclaim: announce early, keep the car steady, then move.

title: Reflections never mean free space
summary: Shine on wet pavement must be verified before it is trusted.
body: Correct answer: read the reflection carefully and keep your speed low.
Why: wet shine can be water, light, or a trick of the angle.
Trap: reflection mistake turns a mirror into a promise.
Reclaim: slow down, verify the surface, and do not guess.
